title: Pricing That Pays: Setting Prices That Are Fair and Profitable
subtitle: Underpricing is not generosity. It is a slow path to closing.

toc.label: Revenue Per Hour: The Hidden Metric
When and How to Raise Prices
Financial Literacy for the Whole Team

sections.heading: The Underpricing Epidemic
sections.body:
Independent cafe owners chronically underprice. They worry that raising prices will lose customers. So they absorb rising costs — rent increases, ingredient inflation, wage growth — and watch margins shrink while working harder.

The irony: the cafes that close are rarely the expensive ones. They are the ones that tried to compete on price with chains that have purchasing power they will never match.

Underpricing signals low value. A flat white at €2.80 makes customers wonder what is wrong with it. The same flat white at €3.80 in a well-designed space with excellent service feels appropriate. At €4.50 with single-origin beans and latte art, it feels premium.

Pricing is not maths alone. It is positioning. Your prices tell customers what kind of cafe you are. If your coffee is specialty, your space is beautiful, and your team is excellent — price accordingly. Anything less is subsidising your customers with your own time and stress.

sections.heading: Cost-Plus vs Value-Based Pricing
sections.body:
**Cost-plus pricing:** Calculate your cost, add a markup, set the price.
- Flat white costs €0.57 to make → apply 4x markup → price at €2.28
- Simple, logical, and often wrong.

Cost-plus ignores what the customer is willing to pay. If your competitor charges €3.80 for the same drink and the customer happily pays it, you are leaving €1.52 on the table.

**Value-based pricing:** Price based on what the customer values, benchmarked against alternatives.
- What do competitors charge for the same drink? (Market reference)
- What experience do you provide alongside the drink? (Ambiance, service, quality)
- What would a customer pay for this without flinching? (Perceived value)

Use T118-01 (Pricing Strategy Worksheet):

| Item | Your COGS | Your Price | Competitor A | Competitor B | Market Avg | Recommended |
|------|-----------|------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| Flat White (reg) | €0.57 | €3.20 | €3.80 | €3.50 | €3.55 | €3.60 |
| Flat White (lrg) | €0.72 | €3.60 | €4.30 | €4.00 | €4.05 | €4.10 |
| Avocado toast | €2.10 | €7.50 | €9.00 | €8.50 | €8.30 | €8.50 |

The recommended price balances your costs, the market, and your positioning. Price at or slightly below market if you are building a customer base. Price at or above market if you are positioned as premium.

**The minimum price formula:**
COGS ÷ target food/coffee cost % = minimum price.
If flat white COGS is €0.57 and your target coffee cost is 20%, minimum price is €0.57 ÷ 0.20 = €2.85. Below that, you lose money.

sections.heading: Revenue Per Hour: The Hidden Metric
sections.body:
Use T119-01 (Revenue Per Hour Dashboard):

Revenue per hour tells you when your cafe is productive and when it is not.

**Revenue per trading hour = Daily revenue ÷ hours open**

A cafe open 12 hours doing €800 = €66.67/hour average. But the average is misleading. The reality:
- 7–9am: €250/hour (peak)
- 9–12pm: €80/hour (steady)
- 12–2pm: €100/hour (lunch)
- 2–5pm: €30/hour (quiet)
- 5–7pm: €20/hour (dead)

The last two hours generate €100 total, but cost you a staff member (€24), utilities, and opportunity cost. Are they worth opening for?

**Revenue per labour hour = Revenue ÷ Total staff hours worked**

This is the efficiency metric. Target: €70+ per labour hour.
- €800 revenue with 3 staff for 10 hours = 30 staff hours = €26.67/labour hour (poor)
- €800 revenue with 2.5 staff average for 10 hours = 25 staff hours = €32/labour hour (better)
- Reduce to 10 hours open, 2 staff = 20 staff hours = €40/labour hour (efficient)

Use hourly data to make decisions:
- Staff more during high-revenue hours (the return justifies the cost)
- Staff less during low-revenue hours (or close earlier)
- Focus marketing and promotions on lifting quiet hours
- Set hourly revenue targets for the team

sections.heading: When and How to Raise Prices
sections.body:
If you have not raised prices in 12 months, you have effectively given yourself a pay cut. Inflation, ingredient costs, and wages have all risen — and your margin has absorbed the difference.

**When to raise prices:**
- Annually, minimum. Tie it to your financial year or a natural moment (new year, new season, new menu).
- When costs increase significantly (supplier price hike, minimum wage increase, rent review)
- When you reposition (menu refresh, renovation, quality upgrade)

**How much to raise:**
- 5–8% annually keeps pace with cost inflation without shocking customers
- Round to clean numbers (€3.20 → €3.50 feels smaller than €3.20 → €3.42)
- Raise everything at once, not item by item over months (one adjustment, one moment)

**How to communicate:**
- **Do not apologise.** "Due to rising costs, we have had to increase..." sounds defensive.
- **Do not over-explain.** Most customers accept reasonable price increases without needing a justification.
- **Do reframe positively.** "We've refreshed our menu with some new additions" alongside the price change.
- **Do maintain value.** If prices go up, make sure quality stays high. A price increase combined with a quality drop is the fastest way to lose customers.

**Who you will lose:**
Almost nobody, if the increase is reasonable. Research consistently shows that 80%+ of customers accept a 5–10% price increase without changing behaviour. The ones who leave over a 30p increase were not your target customer.

The customers who stay — your regulars, your community — stay because of the experience, not the price. Respect them with quality, and they will accept fair pricing.

sections.heading: Building a Budget and Forecast
sections.body:
Use T123-01 (Annual Budget Template):

A budget is a plan for your money. A forecast is your best prediction of what will happen. Most cafes have neither — which means every financial surprise is a crisis.

**Simple annual budget structure:**

| Category | Monthly Budget | Annual Budget | % of Revenue |
|----------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Revenue** | €25,000 | €300,000 | 100% |
| Cost of goods (coffee) | €3,750 | €45,000 | 15% |
| Cost of goods (food) | €5,000 | €60,000 | 20% |
| Cost of goods (other) | €1,250 | €15,000 | 5% |
| **Gross profit** | €15,000 | €180,000 | 60% |
| Labour | €8,750 | €105,000 | 35% |
| Rent & rates | €3,000 | €36,000 | 12% |
| Utilities | €750 | €9,000 | 3% |
| Marketing | €250 | €3,000 | 1% |
| Insurance | €200 | €2,400 | 0.8% |
| Equipment & maintenance | €500 | €6,000 | 2% |
| Other overheads | €500 | €6,000 | 2% |
| **Net profit** | €1,050 | €12,600 | 4.2% |

**Building the budget:**
1. Start with realistic revenue (use last year's data, not wishful thinking)
2. Apply COGS percentages from your actual data
3. Budget labour based on your rota and wage rates
4. Add fixed costs (rent, insurance) — these are known quantities
5. Add variable costs (utilities, maintenance) based on recent history
6. What remains is your projected profit

If the projected profit is negative or too thin, you have three levers:
1. Increase revenue (pricing, footfall, ATV)
2. Reduce COGS (supplier negotiation, portion control, waste reduction)
3. Reduce overheads (renegotiate rent, reduce hours, cut unnecessary spending)

Review actual vs. budget monthly. Variances tell you where to focus.

sections.heading: Financial Literacy for the Whole Team
sections.body:
Use T124-01 (Financial Literacy Workshop Guide) to share financial basics with your team.

**Why the team should understand the numbers:**

A barista who knows the cafe makes 4% net profit understands that every wasted shot, every unrecorded comp, every stolen pastry comes from a very thin margin. Context changes behaviour.

**What to share:**

- **Revenue is not profit.** "We did €1,000 today" does not mean the cafe made €1,000. After costs, the cafe kept €40–60. When they hear this, over-generosity with comps and portions takes on a different meaning.

- **Labour is the biggest cost.** The team should understand that they are the most expensive input. This is not about making them feel guilty — it is about helping them understand why staffing decisions matter and why productivity during peak hours is important.

- **Every item has a cost.** Show them the ingredient cost of a flat white (€0.57). Show them the margin. Then show them what happens when they pour 250ml of milk instead of 200ml, or use 20g of coffee instead of 18g. Small multiplied by frequent equals big.

- **Waste is money in the bin.** €15 of food waste per day = €5,475 per year. That is a barista's monthly wage, thrown away.

**How to share it:**
- One 30-minute session per quarter
- Use real numbers from your cafe (anonymised if you prefer)
- Keep it visual: pie charts of where revenue goes
- Ask questions, do not lecture
- Celebrate improvements: "Our food waste is down 20% since January"

Financial transparency builds ownership. A team that understands the business makes better decisions than one that operates in ignorance.
